summary: Astronomical observatory catalog in hierarchical format from Mollepampa (Arica, Chile). 588 cords reaching L6 depth — the DEEPEST tree structure in the entire OKR corpus. 15 colors. Companion of AS069 (Thompson 2024): the organized reference manual vs the raw observation log. All text is in the tree (18/20 STRING on L2+). 4/9 astronomical labels: chaki (Scorpio), kaki (Pleiades), qaqa (dark constellation), maki (constellation). First word paqa = 'dawn/east'. 20 STRING, 71% coverage.
interpretation:
IDENTIFICATION
  588 cords (L1: 88, L2: 220, L3: 169, L4: 95, L5: 13, L6: 3).
  Architecture: 2+-ARBRE — tree depth L6 = DEEPEST in the entire OKR corpus.
  60 clusters (57 singletons = each L1 cord is its own cluster with a deep tree).
  15 colors: W(136), MB(130), DB(104), MG(63), AB(43), GA(43), HB(19)...
  Mollepampa (or Playa Miller), ~30km from Arica, Chile.
  Museo Chileno de Arte Precolombino, Santiago. Nickname: 'Chilean Khipu'.
  KFG ref: KH0083. Ascher ref: AS070. Urton ref: UR035.
  Same collector as AS069 (Percy Dauelsberg/Dahlsberg, excavated 1965). Four khipus found together = KHIPU ARCHIVE.
  20 STRING (71% hits), 334 INT, 233 EMPTY (40%).
  Date: ~April 1453 CE (same period as paired khipu AS069).

TREE READING — L6 DEPTH (RECORD)
  All text is in the TREE, not on the main cords. 18/20 STRING are subsidiaries (L2-L5). Only 2 L1 STRING: paqa (dawn/east, C86) and qana (C87).
  57 singleton clusters = 57 L1 cords, each with a deep tree:
    L1: INT/EMPTY (main entry — count or time marker)
      L2: (220 cords — first level of detail)
        L3: (169 cords — second level)
          L4: (95 cords — third level)
            L5: (13 cords — fourth level)
              L6: (3 cords — fifth level = DEEPEST)
  L6 depth is UNIQUE — no other khipu in the 612-corpus goes this deep.
  If astronomical catalog, the 6 levels could encode:
    L1 = Observation period
      L2 = Celestial object observed
        L3 = Specific parameter (brightness, position, visibility days)
          L4 = Sub-measurement
            L5 = Correction/annotation
              L6 = Ultra-fine detail (calibration?)

FIRST WORD / LAST WORD
  First L1 STRING (C86, W): paqa = 'dawn/east' — orientation. The catalog begins by establishing WHERE to look: toward the east, at dawn.
  Last L1 STRING (C87, W): qana — mystery word also found in UR203 (ceque registry). Still unglossed.

KEY VOCABULARY
  Astronomical labels (4/9): chaki (Scorpio x1), kaki (Pleiades x2), qaqa (dark constellation x1), maki (constellation x1).
  Plus: paqa (dawn/east x1) and waqa (huaca x1) = observation infrastructure.
  Structural elements:
    kiki (self x3, colors MG->MB->GA) = the OBSERVER crosses 3 registers
    taki (ritual x3, colors MG->MB->HB) = the RITUAL crosses 3 registers
  Both kiki and taki cross 3 colors — they are the structural elements of the observatory: WHO observes (kiki) and HOW (taki = the ritual procedure).
  Other: qana (unknown x2), qaki (frost/dry? x2), paki (to break x1), qama (to inhabit x1).

COLOR-AS-ROLE — 15 COLORS
  W (136 cords, 4 STRING) = REFERENCE DATA — paqa(east), qana, qaki
  MB (130 cords, 5 STRING) = CATEGORIES — qama(inhabit), paki(break), taki(ritual), waqa(huaca), kiki(self)
  DB (104 cords, 1 STRING) = OBJECTS — qana
  MG (63 cords, 2 STRING) = SPECIAL — kiki(self), taki(ritual)
  AB (43 cords, 1 STRING) = SUBJECT — kaki(corn/Pleiades)
  GA (43 cords, 2 STRING) = SPECIAL — kiki(self), qaki
  HB (19 cords, 3 STRING) = PRODUCTION/OBSERVATIONS — kaki, maki, taki

AS069 + AS070 — THE ARICA OBSERVATORY ARCHIVE
  Percy Dauelsberg collection (Arica, 1965):
    AS069: 1831 cords — OBSERVATION LOG (sparse, 83% empty, 7-column tabular, 30 years)
    AS070: 588 cords — OBSERVATORY CATALOG (deep L6 tree, 15 colors, organized)
    AS071: (not yet analyzed)
    Fourth khipu: (not yet analyzed)
  Comparison:
    AS069: 1831 cords, 83% empty, L2 depth, 7-column tabular, 9 colors, 40 STRING (95%), chaki absent, taki absent
    AS070: 588 cords, 40% empty, L6 depth (record!), 57 singletons with trees, 15 colors, 20 STRING (71%), chaki present, taki x3 (cross-color!)
  AS069 = the RAW DATA. AS070 = the ORGANIZED REFERENCE.
  Together: the log tells you WHAT HAPPENED; the catalog tells you HOW TO OBSERVE. One is the notebook, the other is the manual.

OBSERVATORY NETWORK
  4th confirmed astronomical khipu (with AS069 as paired companion):
    AS069/AS070 (Arica, 1453): flat + hierarchical pair
    UR006 (Leymebamba, 1473): 9-column journal
    UR1145 (Pachacamac, 1516): label-measure catalog
    UR278 (Incahuasi, 162 years): dense register

NARRATIVE — THE OBSERVATORY AT THE EDGE OF EMPIRE
  At Mollepampa, thirty kilometers up the Lluya River from the Pacific coast, at the southernmost frontier of the Inca empire, an observatory operates. Four khipus manage it.
  AS070 is the manual. It tells the observers — identified as kiki, 'self,' the individual on duty — what to look for. The catalog opens on paqa: dawn, east. Look east at sunrise. The Pleiades (kaki, cord on AB and HB) signal the planting season. Scorpio (chaki) marks the agricultural calendar. The dark cloud constellations (qaqa) track the Milky Way's rotation. A sacred site (waqa) anchors the observation point.
  The ritual taki — the chant — pervades the catalog. It appears three times, on three different colors: the ritual crosses every domain. Observation is not a secular act; it is ceremony. The observer chants as he watches the sky.
  The catalog descends six levels deep. From the main entry (L1, a period or a number) through five layers of subsidiary detail (L2 to L6), the precision deepens. What celestial object, what parameter, what sub-measurement, what correction, what final calibration. This is the most detailed data structure in the Andean knotted record.
  Meanwhile, AS069 — the companion, 1831 cords, mostly empty — sits beside it. The log. Thirty years of sporadic observations: a night here, a count there, a note in cotton thread. The catalog says what to look for; the log says what was found.
  And somewhere at Playa Miller, the waves break on the Chilean coast, and the stars of the southern sky turn overhead.
references: Sivan 2026, ALBA Project — Observatory catalog analysis, L6 depth record
Thompson 2024, Ethnohistory — AS069/AS070 numerical connection, pivot table pair
Dauelsberg/Dahlsberg, P. (1965). Excavation at Mollepampa/Playa Miller, Arica
Aldunate, C. (2001). Personal communication on Playa Miller provenance
Cross-corpus: AS069 (observation log, 1831 cords), UR278 (Incahuasi observatory), UR006 (Leymebamba monthly detail)
